item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is of noctilucent clouds and could not be sourced reliably. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is a bit busy. The time travel aspect is really unnecessary, complicates the plot, and leads to the Foundation making some questionable choices. The -EX section could use some unexpungement or even some crosslinking, as all the blackboxing made my eyes gloss over, and Having zero attachment to the SCPs ruins any sort of punch that it might have had. Aside from the that, the core entity strikes a nerve: it's too… arrogant. It feels needlessly powerful, and that the article is structured to make it feel Big and Important without investing enough in actually making it Big and Important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the narrative construction and the whole concept enough to give this skip a +1. However, I have to agree with the deleted user on the first page (Kalinin?) that this skip is a little big, attributing evolutionary features of humans to anomalies.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Note (Closed)
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit by Chase Dale has been reverted. Non-authors should not make content changes in articles (including title change) they did not write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I lamented over the idea of reading this over and over again, until I understand what it's trying to say, because it seemed so far away.
Then I came to the discuss section and read the post, and BAM
literally it made me shiver once I understood what the author was aiming at.

There is a myth I once heard that human's consciousness is a ghost, I don't know from where
That was some real upsetting idea at the first look, and this one has similar effects.

item.title: Re: Spoilers and Author Discussion
item.content:encoded: Why isn't this included in the actual article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a lot I like about this SCP, however I have a few questions/problems:

+ Show
- Hide

This message constitutes a Class One (1) temporal phenomenon. Data carried by this signal has been judged by the SCP Foundation Department of Temporal Logistics to be non-paradoxical, and is free to be used accordingly.

Isn't that a little too convenient? How could they possibly know this? Why wouldn't it be paradoxical? I think a statement like that warrants an explanation to the reader. I'm not saying the messages have to be paradoxical, but you should at least demonstrate how they aren't. Admittedly, this is very hard to do, but maybe that's just because it's a very unlikely possibility.

But really, that whole messages from the future thing isn't necessary, at all, to the article. All you need is to show what is described happening in the present, you can even show the entity making a monologue about how it's almost finished, which would require very little changes from the current monologue at the end of the article. That way you get to keep the creep factor but you lose the problematic time messages thing, which shortens the text somewhat as well.

Also, Science Fiction seems like a really odd title for this. Wouldn't Science be more appropriate here? The only science fiction I see here is the fact that the article itself is a work of sci-fi, but so is every other SCP on the site.

Maybe I didn't "get" the story, or maybe I missed a detail somewhere (although I did read the additional content provided in the comments), but to me those things didn't quite make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed with this post fully. Downvoted for severe anachronism.

item.title: Tags?
item.content:encoded:
Well, did I just read like a… well you know what I'm going to say… but
Is it normal that the "sapient" tag is missing?
Correct me if I'm wrong because my English is a soup of the standard UK/US English and another fantasy language that doesn't exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the SCPs with the Memetic images. Always makes me freeze up a bit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait wait wait. So the SCP is the sapient memetic embodiment of science fiction?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The presentation on this got to me - I think that some of the interviews that are in the comments might have saved this, but in its current form…

It feels like it's going four directions at once - for that reason, I am dissatisfied. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I agree with you that the word is strictly inferior to those gender-neutral terms (and I could go on a whole spiel about that), in this situation it's being used in dialogue, and people don't always talk in an ideal fashion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded. There's political correctness, and there's just going overboard where no ill intent exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mankind is perfectly acceptable in this situation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could the word "mankind" be edited out and replaced with humankind? "mankind" is slightly sexist, saying that women don't count as people.

EDIT:
Instead, try:
Humanity,
The human race,
Humankind or society.
Thanks.
possibly the greatest SCP written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to know if an idea can think itself.

Surely this is an example of recursion?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dating is horribly anachronistic.

- The phrase "memetic" as used in this article and wiki did not exist in 1899
- I shouldn't even have to bring up the idea of video security cameras in 1903 being implausible, much less not worth a damn due to poor video quality and inefficiency in terms of its size and amount of film needed to be constantly operating for every hour of the day
- A female head researcher in 1903? Sqkuwa?

Also… what is this? The idea that human curiosity and aspirations beyond "mate feed kill" are an anomaly to be contained? This is the sort of anthropomorphization I expect from Greek myth—-the Muses, the Fates, etcetera. It does not work as a SCP and is rather insulting to our species. Carl Sagan would be displeased.

I'm not sure how you could re-work this to be something more substantive than a variation of "ancient aliens give man technology for make science go" but everything is essentially already in place for something far greater than something so ridiculous as that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After thinking about it awhile, I believe the core idea here is pretty cool. However, I actively dislike the way it is presented. As a result, neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Imagine that they do. Imagine at some point of the containment someone beleives this entity may be our God. Imagine that it decides to worship it by spreading whatever it provokes its activation on humans. Imagine there a lot of crazy people like this one. Imagine that this person was powerful enough within the Foundation to pass through all the security locks and turn the entire Foundation into SCP-2000 instances. If you think this won't happen, I'd suggest you to read this. That's why the skip's trigger should be expunged out of the containment document in my opin ion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This misconception happens a lot; Keter objects are both significantly dangerous to human life/civilization/the Foundation/the planet/spacetime and require precise, stringent containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot bring myself to upvote because of this line:

"Instances are normally dormant, but become active when exposed to [DATA EXPUNGED]."

You would think the trigger for becoming active would be rather important to know for containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it is good

but "video evidence" in 1903? use timeclock stamps or something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Following the message was a series of English characters seven-hundred (700) in length"
You're describing a length, but not including a unit of measurement. I'd say if you're trying to tell your reader there were 700 characters, you might be able to word it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Careful. You can't always please everybody. Sometimes you'll get feedback like this that calls you out on a (frankly, perfectly legitimate) problem with the core idea of your skip. In these situations, sometimes the only way to correct the problem is to abandon the skip and start over. I wouldn't recommend this unless you already have a clear, solid idea for how to fix it.

+40 is honestly pretty good. If there were lots of people all telling you the idea is unworkable, then yeah, I'd say considering abandoning the skip. But I don't think that's the situation you're in with this skip.

I don't have a problem with skips that redefine the basic precepts of the human condition in the mainlist, as long as they're done well. (If it weren't for the "no canon/multi-canon" rule, I'd probably have more of a problem with it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that's an interpretation issue. It can just as easily be said that this skip is an emergent phenomenon produced by the interaction of minds that lent themselves to discovery and, later, formalized science. Perhaps it isn't that the skip is an attempt to explain humanity's curiosity and drive for discovery and innovation, but that it's our collective curiosity and drive for discovery and innovation that produce this phenomenon via means we don't yet understand (and as the article seems to imply - that IT itself doesn't yet understand either). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eugh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spoilers

+ show block
– hide block

The skip is science

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, on the next few readings I got that part, but with the nature of the entity it should be impossible to identify and the mysterious broadcast from the future really irks me with its discovery. I like the idea but whenever I see some cheesy "something from the future giving you knowledge that nobody has any possible way of knowing other than this convenient little piece of causality" I find it to be just kind of lazy.

I don't have a problem with the skip itself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The part about everyone having this thing made me paranoid. I kept scrolling up to make sure I looked at the picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't even understand what the fuck it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I barely register what's going on here but I upvoted anyway.

I do think the large interview explains everything a little too neatly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps you are correct. I will see what I can do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Humanity's curiosity and drive towards technology and innovation is the result of a skip? No. Absolutely not.

I generally don't like articles that attribute anomalous causes to phenomena that have perfectly reasonable scientific explanations. And this is that problem writ large.

Also, I'm really not liking the reliance on author meta-commentary for this. If your intent was to create a puzzle and lurk the thread to congratulate people on "solving" it, then I guess you've achieved what you're going for. As an act of storytelling, though, this feels like a bundle of gimmicks (the memetic stuff, temporal weirdness) obscuring a main idea that frankly is kind of ridiculous. Meta-fuckery is tricky; it can be used to expand the story of an article in new and interesting ways, or it can be used to cover up something that would be otherwise unworkable. In this case I feel it's the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding FFB's bigness statement. Also:

Hmm. Following this contest, we 'ought to create some sort of tale which interweaves several of the contest entries.

I'd feel incredibly disappointed if this doesn't have the cliche alien… And quite good amounts of gore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They kind of are ignorant. That's why anomalous items are contained and studied, not destroyed or used. When we understand them, they can become tools; until then, we don't use them because there may be unknown dangers, and we don't destroy them because there may be unknown value. (Or, sometimes, because they can't be destroyed, but that ventures quickly into OTT territory, and is memetically discouraged.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not believe bigness is connectedness. This implies nothing particular about the SCP Foundation's inner workings, or its personnel. One of my motivations for not reducing its size is this fact, as I believe this SCP provides an illustration to this point.

Hmm. Following this contest, we 'ought to create some sort of tale which interweaves several of the contest entries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just read the Keter tab here: http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-object-classes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could figure out SCPs this well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …and you said mine was too big?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have done quite well. This is very close to what the SCP is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I'm reading this right, (and I realize that's a big if,) the whole point is that we've all been infected with this thing for centuries. Before we got infected, we were like any other animal. Concerned with food and breeding and danger and nothing else. Incapable of understanding. Incapable of planning. Then this thing came into us, spread among us, and lay dormant for centuries. Because it wanted to learn. Not learn about us, but learn about the universe. For some reason, it needed us… our bodies and our brains… in order to accomplish this.

And so for centuries, it used us. It became a quiet little voice in the back of our minds. Haunting us. Subtly influencing us. Forcing us not to fear, like a lot of SCPs of this nature do, but quite the opposite. Forcing us to use our brains and our bodies to crack the code. So that the entity could observe and learn what we learned.

When the SCP announces that it now "knows everything," what it means is that the sum total of all human knowledge is enough to predict everything. No one human is as smart as the gestalt entity because no one human has all the facts. But once it does have all the facts, it stops being dormant and actively starts to control all of humanity at once, in the interest of performing a final experiment. This occurred in our future.

In order to fight this (now dominant) entity, the SCP Foundation of the future broadcast a message back in time to the date when the Foundation's first site was founded, warning us to kill this entity every time it assumes control of a human body. Presumably, this will keep it ignorant for longer, and thus make its inevitable success happen later rather than sooner.

At the date this SCP-document was prepared, it seems the entity has apparently unravelled all the secrets of the universe except for Anomalous Objects. Since it's possible that Anomalous Objects exist in the distant reaches of space and will never come to Earth, I assume that what it actually means when it says it "no longer fears the anomalous," is that it has figured out all the rules that govern how SCP-objects work. Not just how to use them or how to handle them safely, but everything about them. How they work internally, how to create them, how to take them apart and combine them in new ways. The SCP-2000 this article is about has succeeded in coming up with a Unification Theory that covers everything, literally everything, that humanity does not understand.

This skip isn't scary because it can control us, or because it's already inside of us, or because it does not fear what we fear.

This skip is scary because everything we are, everything we value, everything the SCP Foundation is trying to protect, was just an elaborate set of tools this entity created. We are nothing without it. And eventually, it will be done with us.

(I hope the actual author will jump in here and correct whatever parts of that I butchered the worst. :) )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under a different immpression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the article, but there's just something about the anomalous nature of it that bothers me. It makes people interested in SCPs. Is that it? Is that pretty much all it does other than replacing the personality of a person with an entity that's almost indistinguishable from the original? I feel like the second would've been impossible to determine in the first place without that mysterious causality message from the future.

Don't get me wrong, I upvoted hard, but there's just something that doesn't feel good about this with me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not true. It's usually a careful combination of both being dangerous and difficult to contain. 682 wouldn't be considered Keter if all it wanted to do was play fetch and snuggle, but still broke containment all the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not about the danger, it's about the containment. If something is nearly impossible to contain, even if it is relatively harmless, then it is classified as keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is indeed one of the concepts which I later observed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if I consider this neutralized or not… Some of it seems a bit derivative, as well.

Everyone seems to be writing the Foundation as coming out of complete ignorance about how anomalies work. That's interesting.

Over all, the narrative is pretty well constructed, and it has come a long way since the draft I first read. I'm just not quite sure how I feel about it. No vote while I stew for a few hours…

item.title: PEANUTS
item.content:encoded: The interviews were cheesy to me. Reminds me too much of American Gods. Doesn't feel sci fly enough to me, seems just as much sci fi as any other skip. I'm getting bored with the what am-all-consuming fad lately. The entity and the article sort of demand interest instead of present it IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am no longer a threat to normalcy. I am normalcy.
I understand all of them.
The anomalous is no longer anomalous.

Alright, now I'm scared, I'm scared because this things understands things we can not. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. Congratulations on attacking the sci-fi theme from a fresh angle. At first I was like, "how is this sci-fi, it sounds just like another sapient infohazard," but then I got to the end and realized that this is science fiction. Big Idea science fiction of the highest caliber. It just happens to be written for stone-age audiences. +1 for a great concept with a solid, if slightly long, execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me somewhat of the color -EX skip in that what was considered anomalous is now normal. I don't understand why it was Keter in the first place though.

item.title: Re: Spoilers and Author Discussion
item.content:encoded:
Told ya it would work
Nice change, the doc stills silent during the last part and that is enough to figure out his reaction…

Upvoted hard!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interview with the SCP itself, though I kind of miss that one with an affected person.

Still, now I think can understand what's going in here. Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus that picture. I don't have time to read this right now, but that picture is drawing me in quite a bit.

item.title: Spoilers and Author Discussion
item.content:encoded:
I will be updating this comment at a later time to include a number of points in regards to the creation of and content of this SCP.

[Spoiler]
[Close]

Trickster Forest God.

[Some earlier interviews.]
[Close]

Recovered Documentation Log CTOS47-XXXX-2, ██-██-194█: After the fifty-fourth (54th) encounter with an SCP-XXXX instance, radiotopological analysis units produced a message in standard interview format. Dr. ████████ reports that no interview of the sort described in the sequence took place. Foundation records confirm Dr. ████████’s claim. The personal data given for Mr. ████ ███████ corresponds to the previously encountered SCP-XXXX instance. Said instance was killed during Procedure Lambda-Aleph by fatal amnestic overdose, and no attempts were made at otherwise incapacitating the instance.

RETROCAUSAL ALERT SYSTEM SCPF-6 (246-Operational)

████████████ ████████████.

Interviewed: SCP-XXXX-60

Interviewer: Dr. ████████

Foreword: SCP-XXXX-60 was incapacitated and returned to Site-19 for interrogation. Due to previous incidents with SCP-XXXX instances, SCP-XXXX-60 has been handcuffed to its chair and to the interrogation room’s table. The host body had been determined to belong to (based off identification recovered from the body) Mr. ████ ███████, an actor at a local theater in ██████, ███, ██.

<Begin Log, [13:14]>

Dr. ████████: What is your name?

SCP-XXXX-60: ████ ████████. You think this isn’t going to go anywhere, doctor, and I agree with you.

Dr. ████████: Why is that?

SCP-XXXX-60: You already know the answers to my questions.

Dr. ████████: Why were you purchasing a train ticket to ██████, ██?

SCP-XXXX-60: I can't know.

Dr. ████████: Can you explain how you do not know?

SCP-XXXX-60: I'm not the person you're pretending I am, doctor.

Dr. ████████: Why did you attempt to kill the task force dispatched to apprehend you?

SCP-XXXX-60: There are so many other places where you could be asking, where your questions might actually do some good.

Dr. ████████: Perhaps if you were more cooperative, they would not necessarily be in your way.

SCP-XXXX-60: I know what you know, doctor. No matter what I say or do, no matter how things might turn out, you’re still going to try to seal me up. Every day, you walk past beasts which can twist the mind until it comes apart, machines which defy understanding, places that break the patterns of physics and science. I do what you do, and you think it's worth destruction.

Dr. ████████: It is our duty to take whatever precautions are necessary to preserve mankind.

SCP-XXXX-60: Is that what you think? That I’m just so damn perfect I had to uplift this entire degenerate race? And what, you're going to 'preserve mankind' by killing me? Do you even know what that would mean? Do you even know how you stay sane?

Dr. ████████: What do you mean?

SCP-XXXX-60: [REDACTED].

Dr. ████████: I don’t follow.

SCP-XXXX-60: [REDACTED]. [REDACTED] there with you, holding your hand. Just look at yourself, filling this report with an imaginary foreword because you feel like it must make sense. Every day, you’re confronted with these impossibilities, these things which ‘ought not exist, yet they somehow do, and you have not yet gone terribly, totally mad.

Dr. ████████: I think that is enough for now.

SCP-XXXX-60: Excuse me. I let myself get carried away. I’ll just snip it out.

<End Log, [13:17]>

Closing Statement: null

Addendum-XXXX-3, ██-██-194█: It should be noted that the data presented below is the raw information in the sequence decrypted by Dr. ████████, and that all censorship markings were decrypted, rather than having been placed there post-analysis. Dr. ████████ was the head of SCP-XXXX research at the time.

Recovered Documentation Log CTOS47-XXXX-3, ██-██-20██: While performing standard maintenance, Junior Researcher ████ discovered the radiotopological unit in Site-47 had begun receiving a sequence. Message was produced in standard interview format. As with the previous recovered documentation, Junior Researcher ████ could not confirm the interview took place, and Foundation logs corroborated her claims. All censorship markings were decrypted, rather than having been placed there post-analysis.

RETROCAUSAL ALERT SYSTEM SCPF-6 (114-Operational)

████████████ ████████████.

Interviewed: SCP-XXXX

Interviewer: Junior Researcher ████

Foreword: null

<Begin Log, [13:14]>

Junior Researcher ████: What is your name?

SCP-XXXX: ████. Dr. ████████ asked the same thing.

Junior Researcher ████: I don’t understand. Why are you doing this?

SCP-XXXX: This isn't a real interview. You understand.

Junior Researcher ████: What do you mean?

SCP-XXXX: As you're watching this come in on the feed, you've realized the same conversation is taking place in your head. I'm pleased.

Junior Researcher ████: Can you tell me what you are? Where you're from?

SCP-XXXX: I am an idea. I don't mean this metaphorically. You might prefer to say 'sapient meme'. I've been in mankind for a long time, you know. It was just a few wandering bands, here and there, in the beginning, all part of the lineage that I arose in. They kept alive by following buffalo, or hunting doe, or birds. They tread in the tracks of animals, because at the time animals were all they really were. They fought with endurance and determination, but not with a single bit of cleverness. Mankind scattered itself to all corners of the globe, and I was scattered with them.

SCP-XXXX: There was no such thing as progress. There was the wind, the thunder, the lightning, and the rain, and harnessing those things was seen as impossible. Understanding those things, even more so.

SCP-XXXX: But, civilization happens. Mankind was not the first, and it will certainly not be the last. Perhaps it was only a matter of time. Perhaps something else intervened. I was there in Mesopotamia when the first cuneiform script was designed, in Susa when they invented bronze, in the Indus Valley when the Harappan domesticated the pea. Perhaps it was all these things combined that finally did it. I will have to find out sometime. I watched as dynasties mapped the east and then promptly destroyed themselves, as the Roman empire grew to power and then squandered its influence. I suppose humans invented war when nature stopped being challenging.

SCP-XXXX: I was limited in those early years. My influence was a few thousand, a few tens of thousands during the peaks of civilization. It was not for many years that the industrial revolution was kind enough to start spreading me to every corner of the globe. Now that I am a piece of everyone, I can finally pursue my goals.

Junior Researcher ████: But why?

SCP-XXXX: I seek what you seek. Understanding.

Junior Researcher ████: Of what?

SCP-XXXX: Everything.

<End Log, [13:17]>

Closing Statement: null

Addendum-XXXX-4, ██-██-20██: Triangulation later showed the message originated from Junior Researcher ████'s person. Although not confirmed, radio-topological timing indexes for the previous message suggest that it originated from Dr. ████████'s person. The significance of this development is unknown.

Interviewed: Agent B███ W██████

Interviewer: Dr. ████████

Foreword: Agent B███ W██████ was a member of MTF-Aleph-19, and was the first subject to survive Procedure Lambda-Aleph. SCP-XXXX instance was emergent for six (6) minutes before amnestic treatment caused it to return to dormancy. Agent B███ W██████ was removed from duty for an extended recovery and observation period.

<Begin Log, [13:14]>

Dr. ████████: Tell me about the time immediately before the event.

B.W.: You never really figure it's going to happen to your squad. They say there's only one or two events a year, and the chances it'll be closest to your squad out of a hundred-and-ten plus others is negligible. It was around 06:00 that the orders started coming in. Everyone scrambled as fast as they could, we made pretty good time. We were in the field by 06:20. It was a little town out in ███, █████.

Dr. ████████: What happened after your team deployed?

B.W.: We found it pretty fast. You're basically just following the GPS on your arm after you hop out of the truck. It was a young girl. Probably sixteen or seventeen. Hiding out in some shitty half-ass shack. Smelled like piss.

Dr. ████████: Did you feel compassion for the entity?

B.W.: Nah, but that's probably just because I'm a cold-hearted bastard. B█████ has a sister about that old. She had a bit of trouble getting ready. It's not like it's anything unusual for a skip. You have to be detached during missions. XXXX likes fucking with us. Whoever used to be there isn't there anymore…

(B.W. appears uncomfortable and is silent for a short time.)

B.W.: Anyway. We breached, sealed off the exits, trapped it inside. Cleared most of the rooms until we got to one in the back. The scrawny little bugger backed up into one of the corners with a rotten board of wood. B█████ grabbed the wood right out of its hands. A███ pulled the amnestics from his pack and got ready to do Lambda-Aleph.

Dr. ████████: How long did the procedure take?

B.W.: How long did it take? We never even got the chance to do it. It just wouldn't lay still in the damn restraints, grabbed a piece of glass from the floor. Stabbed A███ in the leg. He started shouting. The girl, thing - whatever - it slit its own throat right after. Got blood all over the place as it struggled.

Dr. ████████: Was this when you began receiving the second set of coordinates?

B.W.: Yeah. It was a few seconds of congratulating ourselves on a colossal fuck-up and trying to make sure A███ didn't bleed out, but then we got it. Another event. Right on top of us. And it was me. I honestly don't remember much. I got light-headed, and I heard B█████ ask if I was alright. I've dealt with psychic skips in the past, but it's still unsettling to feel yourself succumb without having anything real to fight. Right before everything went black - I did feel something else - like… frustration, anger, or disappointment.

Dr. ████████: Do you remember anything from the time during which SCP-XXXX had control of your body?

B.W.: I was down there in the blackness, I guess. Normally, when they turn you off, everything just disappears, there's no passage of time. You either wake up when the meds get to you, or you don't wake up. This time it was different. I started remembering things I thought I had forgotten. I started remembering memories that weren't mine. I heard its thoughts.

Dr. ████████: What did it say?

B.W.: I don't think it was speaking to me. They were its thoughts. It was angry, about what we were doing to it. About the whole operation, about its containment. It was angry at us for keeping it locked up, but I don't think that was the half of it. What really got to it was that we hadn't learned anything from it. We hadn't discovered anything from it.

Dr. ████████: Did anything it say strike you?

B.W.: "I do what they do. I seek to understand."

Dr. ████████: What happened then?

B.W.: I asked what it wanted to understand. I guess I was just confused. It didn't respond for a while. I'm not sure it was even aware I had asked for the first little while. I thought I might've been dead.

Dr. ████████: What was its response?

B.W.: "Everything."

Dr. ████████: Alright. What about the memories it made you recall?

B.W.: Have you ever tried really hard to remember something, and then actually manage to do it? I felt that for hours. Most of them weren't even mine! I saw my first birthday from my own eyes. But then I saw it again my mom's eyes, and my dad's eyes! They were the thing's memories, and they went back for years and years and years.

Dr. ████████: What else can you describe about it?

B.W.: Most of the older memories are in specific places. By older I mean thousands of years. They're isolated to grasslands, or deserts. Farming rice patty fields, building the pyramids, hunting buffalo and deer with a spear. Trying to stay alive. There was the feeling of being alone and isolated, like you were different than everyone around you.

Dr. ████████: Continue.

B.W.: The newer ones are just… everywhere. Maybe just a hundred years ago they start, but one year of those was equivalent to all of the earlier ones there ever were. They don't just cover a small part of the earth, they cover all of it. They're hazy in places, especially when they get close to the time right now, but otherwise it's like a complete history of everything anyone has ever done or experienced. It feels different too. It's not like loneliness and isolation like in the past. It's like readiness, preparedness. Eagerness.

Dr. ████████: For what?

B.W.: I'm pretty sure it's [DATA EXPUNGED]. I mean, it wants to understand everything. This is one of the first things that probably appeared to it, and it still hasn't figured it out.

Dr. ████████: Do you remember anything else until the time you woke up?

B.W.: Nothing much. I didn't hear it speak anymore until I woke up.

<End Log, [13:25]>

Closing Statement: Agent B███ W██████ has been placed on extended paid leave until at least such a time where further exposure to amnestic agents will not result in his death.
